text: The surgeon performs a central hepatectomy using a glissonian pedicle approach. The right anterior pedicle and segment IV pedicle are dissected and isolated with a vascular sling. The line of demarcation is marked between segments VI-VII and V-VIII. Parenchymal transection is conducted with an ultrasonic aspirator, clipping and dividing smaller vessels, while larger vessels are dissected, ligated, and divided. Bipolar diathermy is used for hemostasis on the cut surface as the transection progresses deeper and cranially.

Near the hilum, the surgeon dissects and exposes the right anterior pedicle. As the transaction progresses cranially, a plane is created between the right hepatic vein and the middle hepatic vein. On the right side, the pulsations of the exposed right hepatic vein are clearly visible. Stay sutures are placed on the left side for the left side transaction.

The surgeon divides the bridge between the left and right lobes using diathermy, marking the line of transduction on the liver surface. Parenchymal transduction is performed with an ultrasonic aspirator, and the segment IV pedicle is dissected, ligated, and divided. The transection progresses cranially, creating a plane between the middle hepatic vein and the left hepatic vein. The middle hepatic vein is carefully dissected, exposed, and slinged, while the right entry pedicle is clamped and divided.

The surgeon oversews the stump of the right entry pedicle with two layers of 5-0 Prolene sutures. The remaining parenchymal transection is completed, and the sling is removed from the middle hepatic vein, which is then clamped and divided. The specimen is removed, and the stump of the middle hepatic vein is oversewn with two layers of 5-0 Prolene sutures.
